title: With an O and an I
summary: The checked-in CME00096 XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of Wilhelm Heuser's complete article 'With an O and an I,' Anglia 27, new series 15 (1904), pp. 283-319. The article is a heterogeneous edited selection with separate transmission paths: direct transcriptions from Bodleian Rawlinson poet. 175, British Library Cotton Cleopatra B II, Gonville and Caius MS 175/96, and Trinity College Cambridge O.9.38; selected Northern Homily excerpts from BL Harley 4196; passages re-edited from Thomas Wright and Richard Morris; and a Thornton/Cambridge composite chiefly after Carl Horstmann, with George Perry cited. A complete public 99-canvas IIIF facsimile of Trinity O.9.38 is cached and maps the target to canvases 62-63, ff. 54v-56r. Public Bodleian objects for Douce 126 and 128 contain only one non-overlapping opening each, Lincoln's catalog exposes one non-overlapping leaf, and no complete public facsimile was verified for the other direct witnesses. Complete public scans of every identified immediate printed source and the exact DIMEV 1.0 release are cached.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00096
entities.description: The repository artifact consumed by the General Corpus publication workflow and obtained through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME00096
entities.description: U-M's electronic text of Heuser's complete 1904 article, retaining German commentary, all selected Middle English and Latin verse, pagination, notes, and typographic distinctions.

date_statements.label: Publication status
date_statements.value: Public electronic text; exact encoding release date not established

entities.label: Wilhelm Heuser, With an O and an I (1904)
entities.description: The complete article and immediate source encoded by U-M; it combines commentary, direct manuscript transcriptions, selected excerpts, re-edited earlier publications, and comparative quotations.

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, 'With an O and an I,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 27, new series 15 (1904), 283-319.
bibliographic.title: With an O and an I

entities.label: Internet Archive complete Anglia 27 scan
entities.description: Complete public volume scan containing Heuser's article on printed pp. 283-319 / PDF pp. 293-329.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF with metadata and OCR derivatives

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Rawlinson poet. 175
entities.description: Heuser's direct source for the complete Four Evangelists refrain cycle on current f. 132r-v; he records faded and partly erased writing and use of chemical reagent to recover readings.

date_statements.label: Manuscript date
date_statements.value: Second half of the fourteenth century

entities.label: British Library Add MS 45896
entities.description: A collateral eight-stanza Annunciation version associated by DIMEV with the Evangelists cycle. The original vellum roll has been missing since 1948; photographic facsimile MS Facsimile 504 preserves the known reproduction.

entities.label: British Library MS Facsimile 504
entities.description: Photographic facsimile preserving Add MS 45896 before or around its reported loss; no public digital copy was verified.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 4196
entities.description: Heuser's named manuscript source for selected Expanded Northern Homily Cycle passages: Luke f. 6r-v, John ff. 16v-17r, and Matthew ff. 28r-30r.

date_statements.label: Manuscript date
date_statements.value: Fourth quarter of the fourteenth to first quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Tiberius E VII
entities.description: A fire-damaged parallel Expanded Northern Homily Cycle witness cited by Heuser through Horstmann's description, not identified as the manuscript from which Heuser printed his excerpt headings.

date_statements.label: Manuscript date
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

notes: Damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Cleopatra B II
entities.description: Direct manuscript source for Heuser's seven-stanza Middle English poem against the Minorites and ultimate witness behind Wright's 49-stanza Latin antifraternal song.

notes: The target verse miscellany is the fifteenth-century paper component ff. 60-71.

entities.label: Thomas Wright, Political Poems and Songs, volume 1 (1859)

rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval Middle English and Latin textual content represented in the article
rights.basis: The texts are preserved in fourteenth- and fifteenth-century manuscripts and predate modern copyright by centuries. This does not cover modern images, editions, metadata, or provider terms.

rights.component: Heuser's 1904 article, selection, transcription, and editorial commentary
rights.basis: The article was published in 1904, and U-M independently marks the CME item derived from it No Copyright–United States.
The official U-M item states Public Domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: No Copyright–United States is a rights statement, not a worldwide license, and does not transfer to manuscript photographs.

editorial_practices.description: U-M encoded Heuser's complete pp. 283-319 article, including 27 German prose paragraphs and every medieval text group, rather than isolating one poem.

Heuser juxtaposes complete works, selected source-homily passages with omissions, two stanzas from a 49-stanza Latin poem, and short comparative quotations. Completeness therefore must be asserted part by part, not for all cited sources.
For the Douce 126 Passion song and Douce 128 prayer, Heuser follows Morris but rearranges or infers stanza form from manuscript layouts he regards as distorted or incomplete.
editorial_practices.scope: Passion song and prayer, printed pp. 303-305
notes: The exact manuscript lineation and layout require target facsimiles.

editorial_practices.description: Heuser prints the Thornton Adam version essentially after Horstmann while adding substantive Cambridge Dd.5.64 variants and citing Perry; his article is not a direct diplomatic transcription of either manuscript.
editorial_practices.scope: When Adam delved and Eve span, printed pp. 305-308

editorial_practices.source_feature: Very faded and partly erased Rawlinson writing
editorial_practices.representation: Heuser marks unresolved readings in brackets after attempting recovery with chemical reagent
editorial_practices.description: Heuser reports using chemical reagent on Rawlinson f. 132 to recover faded readings. Modern verification should use multispectral or conservation-approved imaging rather than reproduce this historical intervention.

Five Latin hour or display headings inside the Hours are encoded as L elements. The manifest separates the raw 101 L representation from 96 Middle English verse lines to avoid treating headings as verse.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00096.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00096 and preserves Heuser's complete article, pagination, hierarchy, commentary, verse, notes, and typography.

evidence.citation: Reproducible namespace-aware structural and language audit of CME00096.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 2 DIV1, 6 DIV2, 13 DIV3, 124 HEAD, 27 P, 99 LG, 1,001 L, 37 PB, 36 MILESTONE, and 87 NOTE; verifies printed pp. 283-319, 804 Middle English L and 197 Latin L, and five non-verse Latin hour/display headings.

open_questions.question: Why do Morris, Heuser, and CME00096 display twenty short lines for the Douce 128 prayer while DIMEV describes sixteen?
next_steps: Obtain Douce 128 f. 258 and inspect original lineation.
Compare DIMEV segmentation and later editions before amending either count.

open_questions.question: How do Wright and Heuser's historical f. 59 locator for the Latin Cotton Cleopatra B II poem and the current catalog component beginning at f. 60 reconcile?
next_steps: Request target images or a current foliation concordance from the British Library.
Do not infer an offset from the English poem's historical/current locator alone.

open_questions.question: Can whole-object facsimiles be obtained for Rawlinson poet. 175, Harley 4196, Cotton Cleopatra B II, Douce 126, Douce 128, Lincoln MS 91, CUL Dd.5.64, and Caius 175/96?
next_steps: Use the recorded institution-specific request routes and ask first whether a complete preservation surrogate already exists.
Request complete codices where feasible and identify the target folios separately.
Preserve written reuse terms before placing any supplied image in Git or print-on-demand outputs.

open_questions.question: Can the Latin defense of friars in Trinity O.9.38 be attributed more securely than Heuser's discussion of Richard FitzRalph and Richard Kilmington permits?
next_steps: Transcribe and search the full text against current Latin repertories.
Treat FitzRalph and Kilmington as subjects or contextual names until attribution evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Which cached or future manuscript images may be redistributed through a repository that also supports commercial print-on-demand use?
next_steps: Keep all source-cache files Git-ignored and publish URLs, locators, and checksums only by default.
Treat Bodleian and Trinity CC BY-NC images as noncommercial; obtain separate commercial permission before inclusion in for-profit products.
Obtain written terms from BL, Lincoln, Caius, and CUL before redistributing newly supplied images.

review_notes: The source graph deliberately separates direct manuscript transcription, excerpting, dependence on earlier editions, citation, and modern catalog description.
The lost Add MS 45896 version is collateral evidence discovered and published after Heuser; it is not a retroactive source of the 1904 article.
A complete published digital object can still be only a selected image set: the Douce bundles are complete representations of their one-canvas public objects, not of the codices.
All cached files are Git-ignored; each is hardlinked into both the CME00096 research worktree and the main checkout source-cache with matching inode and checksum.
